one_line_takeaway: A training-free RAG + LLM pipeline that turns HAR into retrieval-augmented reasoning, reaching or beating supervised DL SOTA on 5 of 6 wearable-sensor benchmarks at ~$0.0006 per prediction and uniquely able to name unseen activity classes — but it is a pervasive-computing paper with no security dimension.
key_finding: Training-free RAG-HAR matches or beats supervised DL SOTA on 5 of 6 datasets while doing zero task-specific training. In the ablations, the LLM reasoning layer adds 14-19% F1 over the retriever alone, prompt optimization adds a further 2-5%, and LLM-based descriptors add ~6% on the hardest dataset (USC-HAD), taking it from a retriever-only 40.1% to 68.2% F1 with all enhancements. It also outperforms the MTMD open-set baseline by 3-7% F1 across openness levels and can generate coherent labels for unseen classes (96.47% for one withheld class, degrading to 88.90% for three).
core_contribution: RAG-HAR is a training-free, retrieval-augmented framework that repurposes pretrained LLMs as reasoning engines for wearable-sensor Human Activity Recognition (HAR), eliminating the dataset-specific model training that dominates DL-based HAR. Its own contribution has two stages. (1) Baseline: each sliding window is Z-score normalized, sub-divided into Full/Start/Mid/End partitions, and reduced to 8 statistical descriptors per channel (mean, max, min, Q1, Q3, std, median, peak count); these are serialized into text strings, embedded with a general-purpose text-embedding model, and stored in a vector database with metadata. At inference, an ANN (cosine) search plus weighted re-ranking retrieves the top-q labeled examples, which are packed into a structured prompt so the LLM classifies the query window grounded in retrieved neighbors. (2) Optimized stage adds two orthogonal enhancements the authors design themselves: an evolutionary/roulette-wheel prompt optimizer (meta-prompt loop over candidate system instructions, F1 as fitness) and LLM-generated natural-language activity descriptors that replace the raw statistical templates to yield semantically richer embeddings for retrieval. A distinctive claimed capability is open-world HAR: detecting unseen activity classes and generating human-interpretable labels for them without retraining.
threat_model: N/A — this is not a security paper. It is a pervasive-computing / machine-learning paper on sensor-based activity recognition. There is no adversary, attack surface, or defense; no security threat model should be forced onto it. The 'open-set / unseen activity' evaluation concerns novel (out-of-distribution) benign activity classes, not adversarial inputs.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) reducing wide sensor windows (up to 60 channels in SKODA) to statistical descriptors to fit the LLM context sacrifices fine-grained temporal patterns and inter-channel dependencies; (2) reliance on a general-purpose text-embedding model forces a lossy statistics-to-text conversion because no general time-series embedding model exists. Reviewer-observed: the baseline does NOT actually reach SOTA on all six — on USC-HAD baseline F1 (58.63%) is below the Triplet-LSTM HTL-SB prior (62.80%), and even the fully optimized USC-HAD result (68.2%) is only shown in the ablation figure, not the head-to-head SOTA table; on HHAR the margin over Enhanced CPC (59.86 vs 59.25) is razor-thin. Gains are described by the authors themselves as 'modest' on several datasets. The method depends on proprietary closed models (gpt-5-mini, gpt-5, OpenAI embeddings) and a commercial vector DB, raising reproducibility and non-determinism concerns (partly mitigated by retrieval grounding). Prompt optimization is a one-time offline cost but requires a validation set and is dataset-specific. Unseen-label accuracy is reported with very large variance (78.0 ± 19.8%).

datasets_used: PAMAP2 (36 of 52 channels retained; 12 activities, 8 subjects; 100Hz, 2s/0.5s windows)
GOTOV (9 channels; 16 activities; 35 older adults >61; 24-sample windows)
USC-HAD (6 channels acc+gyro; 12 activities, 14 subjects; downsampled 33.3Hz, 1s windows; subjects 13-14 held out)
quantitative_results: PAMAP2: RAG-HAR F1 91.12% / Acc 91.60% vs best prior Triplet-LSTM(OTL) 90.40% F1
MHEALTH: RAG-HAR F1 96.74% / Acc 96.91% vs best prior ADFE 96.47% F1
GOTOV: RAG-HAR F1 79.92% / Acc 81.55% vs best prior ALAE-TAE-CutMix+ 79.40% F1
SKODA: RAG-HAR F1 95.21% / Acc 96.04% vs best prior ALAE-TAE-CutMix+ 94.80% F1
HHAR: RAG-HAR F1 59.86% / Acc 58.61% vs best prior Enhanced CPC 59.25% F1
USC-HAD (baseline): RAG-HAR F1 58.63% / Acc 57.20%, BELOW prior Triplet-LSTM(HTL-SB) 62.80% F1
Overall claim: +0.5% to +6.2% F1 over leading DL baselines; new SOTA in 5 of 6 datasets
LLM reasoning adds 14-19% F1 over retriever-only; prompt optimization adds 2-5%; LLM descriptors add ~6% on USC-HAD
Open-set F1 (RAG-HAR vs MTMD): 30% unknown 88.75 vs 82.37; 50% 77.39 vs 70.33; 70% 69.92 vs 67.05
Unseen label availability (PAMAP2, leave-one-class-out): true label present acc 78.0 ± 19.8%, label hidden 63.2 ± 20.7% (~15% drop)
Multiple-unknown labeling accuracy: 1 class 96.47%, 2 classes 92.73%, 3 classes 88.90%
Per-class unseen labeling: standing 96%, walking 94%, running 84%, lying 82%, sitting 76%, ascending stairs 76%, descending stairs 67%
Cost (MHEALTH): indexing 4,000 samples $0.0176; one-time offline prompt optimization $12.56; 600 predictions $0.3739 = $0.000623 amortized per sample
